articles.title: About me
articles.contents:
I’m a neuroscientist currently living in Denver, Colorado, and relocating to the DC area soon to pursue postdoctoral training at the NIH. I grew up in Corvallis, Oregon, and Boise, Idaho, and came to Denver to pursue a PhD in Neuroscience at the University of Colorado, Anschutz Medical Campus. My research is described on my Research page. My career goal is to become an independent investigator in neuroscience and to teach statistics courses for researchers in the biological sciences (Why?).
During my undergraduate education I studied math and biology and worked full-time tutoring, TAing, and teaching math courses for 3 years. I’m passionate about math education and dismantling the idea that math isn’t for everyone. In addition to being a postdoc in Neuroscience, I’m a part-time graduate student (yes, more grad school) slowly pursuing an MS in Statistics so that I can make math and stats education for biologists part of my academic career.
Outside of the lab and classroom, I’m an avid distance runner, cat mom, amateur artist, and board game enthusiast. And, unlike this paragraph, I don’t compartmentalize or make any real attempt to separate these parts of my life from the science part. I built this website with the R package {Distill}, which you can learn more about here.

articles.title: Cauchy & Misc
articles.description: Yes, welcome, this is a whole page of my cat and links to miscellaneous things I like.
articles.contents:
Contents
Cauchy
Improving science (with links to elsewhere)

Cauchy
Cauchy’s response to “Foods?”This is Cauchy, named for this guy. I adopted him as a kitten from a shelter on my birthday several years ago. Words Cauchy currently responds to are his name (and also variants “Cauch” and “Mr. Cauch”), “foods”, “treats”, and “drugs” (catnip).
Sometimes he gets haircuts!Sometimes he plays games!Usually he interrupts work.Improving science (with links to elsewhere)
I like these pieces that introduce some problems in academic science and propose possible solutions.
Organization of academic science:
The creation and waiting room-ness of the modern postdoc position: Can Twitter Save Science? by Alex Danco
The one PI - one lab model: Academic precarity and the single PI model by Romain Brette
If we value mentorship, it should be taught and evaluated:
Scientists aren’t trained to mentor. That’s a problem. By Adam Ruben for Science
Ben Barres proposed a mentoring index for PIs in his 2013 piece on choosing a graduate advisor. Ben argued for assessing trainee h-indices, but well-mentored trainees aspire to careers both within and outside of academia. An alternative mechanism might be to survey lab member training satisfaction with career-independent questions and to use responses to inform “Investigator” scores on NIH grants, for example.
Stats misunderstanding and misuse is widespread in the biological sciences:
An accessible introduction to the problem by Tom Siegfried for Nautilus
Especially prevalent in neuroscience:
Nieuwenhuis et al., 2011
Button et al., 2013

articles.title: Running
articles.description: Running for fun in Denver, CO.
articles.contents:
Contents
Miles in 2019-2020
Racing

Fall colors on 7th St in the Congress Park neighborhood.I started running in early 2017. When I started, I needed to stop every 0.25-0.5 mile to walk. I followed the 10% rule and have worked up to an average 35-40 miles per week for the last few years. Running is an outlet and it’s important to me that I keep it fun. I don’t wear a GPS (prefer perceived effort) and instead map distances post-run with Footpath, which I highly recommend.
Footpath map of a recent good long run around Washington ParkMiles in 2019-2020
I started tracking weekly distances mid-2018, so I now have two full years of run data (2019, 2020). Here are some weekly data:







# A tibble: 52 x 3
    week miles_2019 miles_2020
   <dbl>      <dbl>      <dbl>
 1     1       33.9       38.2
 2     2       34         40.4
 3     3       24.8       40.8
 4     4       34.6       41.8
 5     5       37.8       41.7
 6     6       34.7       41.2
 7     7       42.4       39.7
 8     8       40.1       41.2
 9     9       40.4       36.6
10    10       27.5       42.4
# ... with 42 more rows



running_long <- running %>% 
  pivot_longer(!week, names_to = "year", values_to = "miles")







# A tibble: 1 x 2
  `total miles in 2019` `total miles in 2020`
                  <dbl>                 <dbl>
1                 2045.                 2026.

Racing
I don’t really race, and especially not in the past year. Still, I have a hard time keeping track of the outcomes when I do, so I’ll keep PR’s here.
5k PR: 20:15 (6:53 min/mi), 5/4/2019 National MS Society 5K at City Park, Denver, CO. 1st F / 4th overall.
10k PR: 42:39 (6:52 min/mi), 12/7/2019 Santa Stampede in Littleton, CO.
10 mile: 1:13:49 (7:23 min/mi), 1/25/2019 Frosty’s Frozen 5 and 10 at Hudson Gardens in Littleton, CO. 4th F.
Half marathon: 1:36:05 (7:20 min/mi), 2/22/2020 Snowman Stampede at Hudson Gardens in Littleton, CO. 4th F.
Full marathon? TBD!
National MS Society 5k, May 2019

articles.title: Gallery
articles.description: I started painting more regularly at the onset of the pandemic. Most of these are acrylic on stretched canvas, unless otherwise noted.
articles.contents:
Contents
Zebrafish art
Microglia art
Oligodendrocyte art
Not science art

My partner and I had covid-19 a couple weeks before it was reported that cats could get sick, too.
Cauchy screams (or yawns)
Zebrafish art
The zebrafish spinal cord, viewed laterally but imagined underwater, complete with octodendrocytes and a seafloor plant Mauthner.
Zebrafish embryos in chorions!Adult zebrafishCan you spot the zebrafish? A Denver zebrafish starry night. 
Geometric zebrafish bbsMicroglia art
Tectal microglia, here isolated (n=9 cells from each of 9 larvae) and below hugging neuronal somas. 
Tectal microglia and neurons!Oligodendrocyte art
Bought a clock kit to timelapse-image oligodendrocytes at home! A sheath per hour is about 2.4 fold slower than this occurs in fish (5 hours for all sheaths). It’s always oligo’clock! 
Making more functional things. These are glia coasters (L to R): NG2, radial glia, microglia, oligodendrocyte. 
When I started writing my dissertation (esp acknowledgments!) it was evident that I was going to need tissues. 
Oligodendrocytes without axons (first attempt)
Oligodendrocytes and axons, playing with depth (much improved)
Not science art
FlowersTrianglesA little too much time to reminisce during WFH. Lucy and Pearl, my pet birds (cockatiels) that I had as a kid. 
I cut a couple square canvases and painted them as Zoom frames for a very 2020 Halloween.

articles.title: Research & Publications
articles.description: Past and present research in neuroscience and developmental biology.
articles.contents:
Contents
Overview
Graduate research
Previous work & undergraduate research

Overview
I use zebrafish to investigate development and function of glial cells of the central nervous system. Zebrafish are small, transparent vertebrates that develop quickly. Here is one at 24 hours post-fertilization (hpf). I’ve labeled the heart with green fluorescent protein.
Zebrafish at 24 hpfGraduate research
I did my graduate training in Bruce Appel’s lab at the University of Colorado Anschutz Medical Campus and defended my PhD in Neuroscience in September 2020. I pursued two separate projects in the lab: in the first, I investigated the localization and function of synaptic proteins in oligodendrocytes, and in the second I discovered that microglia prune myelin sheaths during development.
Hughes AN & Appel B (2019). Oligodendrocytes express synaptic proteins that modulate myelin sheath formation. Nature Communications, 10(1), 1-15.
Paper link
PDF

Hughes AN & Appel B (2020). Microglia phagocytose myelin sheaths to modify developmental myelination. Nature Neuroscience, 23, 1055–1066.
Paper link
PDF

I had the opportunity to write a blog piece for The Node about the story behind the microglia story, Retracting sheaths & words
Open Box Science invited to me give a presentation on the microglia paper, which is available to view on Youtube
My dissertation
Microglia exhibit frequent, large calcium transients during developmentMicroglia in the larval optic tectumPrevious work & undergraduate research
During my first year of graduate school, I wrote a journal club-style review about the relationship between Schwann cells and motor neuron axon arborization with my peers Alison Hixon and Megan Josey.
Hughes AN, Hixon AM, Josey M (2016). A Schwanncentric View of Axon Arborization in Neuromuscular Junction (NMJ) Formation. Journal of Neuroscience, 36(38), 9760-9762.
PDF

I performed undergraduate research in Julie Oxford’s lab at Boise State University. Julie’s lab studies collagens, a family of extracellular matrix proteins produced by chondrocytes during long bone development. My research project investigated how the minor fibrillar collagen chain encoded by Col11a1 functions in the endoplasmic reticulum (ER) of chondrocytes during development. During my time in the lab, I wrote two reviews about ER unfolded protein response (UPR) signaling, one in the context of bone development and the other in the context of liver development (with consultation from Dr. Kristen Mitchell, also at BSU).
Hughes AN, Oxford A, Tawara K, Jorcyk C, Oxford JT (2017). Endoplasmic reticulum stress and unfolded protein response in cartilage pathophysiology; contributing factors to apoptosis and osteoarthritis. International Journal of Molecular Sciences, 18(3), 665.
PDF

Hughes AN & Oxford JT (2014). A lipid-rich gestational diet predisposes offspring to nonalcoholic fatty liver disease. Hepatic Medicine, 6, 15-23.
PDF
